coredata.dc:title: Big-small size design of carbon-based multilayer films for improved wear life
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Li
Zhu, Hang
Yang, Jingyuan
Lv, Jianxiang
Zhang, Xingkai
Bai, Changning
coredata.dc:description: Anti-wear is an essential prerequisite for the reliability and durability of silicon-based micro/nano devices operation. To design lubricating films suitable for micro/nano devices, big-small size carbon composite multilayer films were introduced to improve wear life, which is called grain size refining effect of multilayer films. Specifically, graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were alternately spin-coated to create (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films as solid lubricant. The results show that (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer film exhibits superior wear resistance with the wear life of 9400 s under a high load of 4 N. The service life of (GO/CQDs)5 film is more than six times than that of (GO)5 film. The excellent friction performance of (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer is attributed to the layered 3D structure composed of GO nanosheets alternate with CQDs, in which the rigid GO nanosheets act the role in load-carrying capacity, and CQDs could be helpful to enhance deformation resistance of film. Meanwhile, friction-induced structural transformation makes part GO convert into graphene on sliding interface, which is benefit for reducing friction. It is expected that such multilayers will be good lubricating films for silicon-based micro/nano devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene oxide
Carbon quantum dots
Multilayer film
Loading capacity
Wear life

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51765036 ) and the Foundation of Liaoning Key Laboratory of Aero-engine Material Tribology (Grant No. LKLAMTF202303 ) for financial support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the constructing process of (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) TEM image, and (d) HRTEM image of GO.
(a) FTIR spectrum, (b) Raman spectrum, (c) TEM image, and (d) HRTEM image of CQDs.
SEM cross section morphology of different films: (a) (GO/CQDS)1, (b) (GO/CQDS)2, (c) (GO/CQDS)3, (d) (GO/CQDS)4, (e) (GO/CQDS)5, and (f) (GO)5.
AFM morphology of the films over a scanning range of 20 µm × 20 µm: (a) (GO/CQDS)1, (b) (GO/CQDS)2, (c) (GO/CQDS)3, (d) (GO/CQDS)4, (e) (GO/CQDS)5, and (f) (GO)5.
(a) XPS survey spectra of (GO/CQDs)n (n = 1,3,5) films, and C 1 s fine spectra of (b) (GO/CQDS)1, (c) (GO/CQDS)3, (d) (GO/CQDS)5.
(a) Raman spectra of (GO/CQDs)n (n = 1–5) films, and Raman spectrum of (b) (GO/CQDS)1, (c) (GO/CQDS)2, (d) (GO/CQDS)3, (e) (GO/CQDS)4, and (f) (GO/CQDS)5.
Friction coefficient curves for different films at sliding frequency of 1 Hz and load of 4 N.
SEM images of the wore surfaces on different films: (a) (GO/CQDs)1, (b) (GO/CQDs)2, (c) (GO/CQDs)3, (d) (GO/CQDs)4, (e) (GO/CQDs)5, (f) (GO)5, and (g) Wear rate of (GO/CQDs)n and (GO)5 films.
(a) friction curve of (GO/CQDs)5 film, (b) Raman intensity map of (GO/CQDs)5 film at different friction times, (c) Raman spectra of the wear scar area of (GO/CQDs)5 film at different rubbing times.
Schematic diagram of friction mechanism of (GO/CQDs)5 films.

head.ce:title: Big-small size design of carbon-based multilayer films for improved wear life

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aSchool of Petrochemical Technology, Lanzhou University of Technology, Lanzhou 730050, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Wear and Protection of Materials, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Science and Technology on Wear and Protection of Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKey Laboratory of Science and Technology on Wear and Protection of Materials, Lanzhou Institute of Chemical Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Lanzhou 730000, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Anti-wear is an essential prerequisite for the reliability and durability of silicon-based micro/nano devices operation. To design lubricating films suitable for micro/nano devices, big-small size carbon composite multilayer films were introduced to improve wear life, which is called grain size refining effect of multilayer films. Specifically, graphene oxide (GO) nanosheets and carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were alternately spin-coated to create (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films as solid lubricant. The results show that (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer film exhibits superior wear resistance with the wear life of 9400 s under a high load of 4 N. The service life of (GO/CQDs)5 film is more than six times than that of (GO)5 film. The excellent friction performance of (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer is attributed to the layered 3D structure composed of GO nanosheets alternate with CQDs, in which the rigid GO nanosheets act the role in load-carrying capacity, and CQDs could be helpful to enhance deformation resistance of film. Meanwhile, friction-induced structural transformation makes part GO convert into graphene on sliding interface, which is benefit for reducing friction. It is expected that such multilayers will be good lubricating films for silicon-based micro/nano devices.

ce:list-item.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Microelectromechanical systems (MEMS), which promise high integration, intelligence and multifunctionality, emerge as key technologies for technological development, economic prosperity and national defense security [1,2]. Due to microminiaturization endowing large specific surface area, a requirement to assemble components with suitable mechanical stability in MEMS, is to enable relative motion surface with excellent anti-wear ability [3,4]. The operational efficiency of MEMS is assuredly governed by this attribute, which, in turn, is determined by intrinsic properties, morphology characteristics and other factors of friction layer materials. Ideal contact surface minimizes adhesion and friction force for achieving low wear by regulating chemical composition and providing surface roughness features at the nanoscale [5].

To date, the film material selection for motion surface is still limited to a few ceramics and silicon, most predominantly silicon nitride and monocrystalline silicon [2,6]. Other films such as Langmuir-Blodgett film and self-assembled film exist as niche products, but are considered as potential protective channel to solve wear problems of MEMS component surface [7,8]. The latter overcome the difficulties of preparing dense, uniform, and controllable nanofilms on complex substrate surfaces, and avoiding the use of special equipment and complex processes. The characteristics with in-situ spontaneous formation, high thermodynamic stability, high coverage, and surface structures artificially designed to obtain the expected physical and chemical properties of the interface, are received close attention from scientific researchers [9].

Graphene, a two-dimensional layered material composed of single carbon atoms, displays high thermal conductivity, corrosion resistance, mechanical strength and low shear force, which is one of the preferred materials in the self-assembly process [10–13]. However, graphene is difficult to form powerful bonds on solid surfaces due to the few active groups on its surface. Compared with inert graphene, graphene oxide (GO) contains abundant oxygen-containing groups, and could be easily modified with various molecules [14–16] as some silane coupling agents, and chemically adsorb on a variety of substrates [17–21]. Routinely, graphene oxide films containing organic molecules are directly used to improve anti-friction ability and wear life, but it still does not meet the requirements of solid lubrication films for micro-nano devices, and the prominent points are the load-bearing capacity and wear life.

Based on the points, inorganic nanomaterials with high hardness and good friction-reduction were introduced to GO based films to enhance the loading capacity and friction performance [22]. Molybdenum disulfide (MoS2)/GO composite film was constructed by spraying the MoS2 solution and GO solution, in which the friction coefficient was about 0.06 and the wear life was 240 min under the conditions of 10 N and 200 rpm/min in N2 environment, but the wear life was only about 30 min in air under the same conditions. CeO2 was employed to prepare CeO2/GO composite film on silicon substrate by self-assembly method. The friction coefficient of the film decreased dramatically compared with the bare silicon substrate, and the wear life of the film was more than 7 times than that of GO film [23]. Although the wear resistance of CeO2/GO film has been improved significantly, the bearing capacity of the film cannot meet the requirements of long-term stability and high reliability for MEMS and other microdevices.

Here, we propose a new strategy to create multilayer films mainly composed of carbon atoms with high friction stability and long wear life. We realize that big-small size carbon materials composite systems may be ideally suited to achieve long wear life, called grain size refining effect of multilayer films. We form the multilayer films by spin coating method of tailored colloidal dispersions consisting of GO and carbon quantum dots (CQDs), respectively. CQDs are mainly composed of carbon core and surface groups including hydroxyl and carboxyl groups, and commonly employed to prepare composite lubricant additive for liquid lubrication [24]. Like, CQDs/Cux composite material was used as a lubricant additive in liquid paraffin. Compared with the pure liquid paraffin, paraffin oil with the CQDs/Cux lubricant additive obviously reduced the friction coefficient and wear rate of AISI52100 steel ball, which is attributed to the synergistic effect of CQDs and Cux nanoparticles and the graphic fragment structure characteristic of CQDs [25]. PMMA/CQDs composite as a lubricant additive was synthesized under mild conditions by one-step method. The VG 200 lubricating oil with 0.3 wt % PMMA/CQDs can greatly reduce friction and wear. The friction and wear of X210Cr12 steel disc are reduced by 20.2 % and 41.6 %, respectively. The PMMA/CQDs composite shows high anti-friction and anti-wear properties, which is mainly because the entrapped CQDs were released from the polymer structure under lubrication conditions and acted as the bearing balls to improve the ability of friction-reducing and anti-wear by reducing the contact area between the friction pairs [26]. A paramount concern is whether CQDs used in solid lubricating film could still provide good lubrication.

In this study, we focus on the friction performance of (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films in macroscales. It is gratifying that the combination between robust mechanical properties of GO nanosheets and high friction-reducing ability of CQDs would improve the loading capacity and wear life. It is anticipated that the obtained solid lubricant film is suitable for MEMS and micro/nanodevices.

N-type monocrystalline silicon wafers were purchased from MCL Electronic Materials Co., Ltd. Natural graphite powder (100 mesh) was supplied by Qingdao Huatai Lubricating and Sealing Technology Co., Ltd. (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES), tannic acid and ethylenediamine were obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Sodium nitrate, concentrated sulfuric acid (98 %), phosphoric acid, potassium permanganate, hydrogen peroxide (30 %), and anhydrous ethanol were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All materials were used directly without further purification. Ultrapure water (≥18 MΩ) was used throughout the experiment.

Graphene oxide was synthesized from graphite powder by a modified Hummers method [27]. The details of the preparation procedures are given in Supplementary information (See SI1). Subsequently, dried GO was exfoliated by sonication (400 W) for 1 h to obtain GO colloid solution.

CQDs was synthetized according to the reports [28] and improved by ourselves. The details of the preparation procedures are given in Supplementary information (See SI2). The obtained CQDs powder was prepared into 1 mg/ml aqueous solution for later use.

Film fabrication process is visualized in Fig. 1 . Firstly, silicon wafers were immersed in Piranha solution for 30 min to obtain hydroxylated silicon wafers, which were then washed and placed in 3 % APTES acetone solution for 30 min. After cleaning thoroughly with ultrapure water and drying with nitrogen, the film designated as APTES-SAM was obtained, and the preparation process was shown as step 1 and 2 in Fig. 1. Subsequently, GO colloid solution and CQDs solution were spin-coated on the modified Si wafers, respectively, to obtain (GO/CQDs)1 films (as shown in steps 3 and 4 in Fig. 1). Repeat step 3 and 4 to obtain (GO/CQDs)2 films, and repeat the above operation to obtain (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films (n = 1–5). The detailed preparation method is given in Supplementary information (See SI3).

Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR), transmission electron microscopy (TEM), high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscope (AFM), Raman spectrometer and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to characterize the multilayer film system and structure evolution. Experimental details of the characterization techniques used in this study are described in the Supplementary information (See SI4).

The tribological properties of the thin films were measured using a CSM tribometer (Anton Paar TRB3 tribometer) in a ball-on-plate reciprocating mode. The upper counterpart was a commercially available GCr15 steel ball (Ф 6 mm) with hardness of 62−65 HRC and announced mean roughness of Ra∼0.12 µm, and the prepared film as lower sample was fixed on a flat base and kept reciprocating with 5 mm. The load of 4 N and the frequency of 1 Hz (10 mm/s) were applied. To compare with previous results obtained by different tribometer or under diverse experimental conditions, the initial maximum Hertz contact pressure in this work is calculated by Hertz contact stress formula [29]. When the applied load is 4 N, the initial maximum Hertzian contact pressure is about 953 MPa, which is larger than the reported data (860 MPa) [30]. The above tests were conducted at a temperature of 25 ℃ and a relative humidity of 30 %−40 %. The worn surfaces of the samples were analyzed using SEM. The wear volumes of the samples were measured using a laser confocal 3D profiler (RTEC). All tests were conducted at least 3 times to ensure the accuracy of the experimental results.

The FTIR spectrum of GO powder (Fig. 2a ) shows that various oxygen-containing groups exist on the surface of GO. The absorption bands at 3344, 1772, 1621, 1410, 1220 and 1053 cm−1 can be assigned to −OH vibration from −COOH and H2O, C=O vibration, C=C vibration, O−H vibration, C−O vibration of C−OH groups, and epoxy vibration, respectively [27]. As shown in Fig. 2b, GO showed clear D and G peaks at 1352 and 1600 cm−1, respectively, which are the primary feature of the Raman spectra of GO sheets [31]. The G peak has shifted slightly, which may be due to the high oxidation degree of the GO powder we synthesized [32]. TEM image (Fig. 2c) shows that GO nanosheets have high transparency and some wrinkles at the edge, while HRTEM image (Fig. 2d) shows that the interlayer spacing has increased to 1.01 nm. This is because the graphite powder was intercalated and oxidized when prepared by improved Hummers method, which leads to the increase of interlayer spacing.

Fig. 3a shows the FITR spectrum of CQDs, from which it can be seen that CQDs powder contains the same functional groups including OH, C=O, C=C, C−O−C, C−OH as GO powder, and these active groups enable them to perform good chemical reactions [32,33]. The Raman spectrum of CQDs (Fig. 3b) also shows obvious D and G peaks as that of GO powder. From the TEM image (Fig. 3c), it can be seen that the prepared CQDs powder presents a spheroidal shape. The inset in Fig. 3c shows the particle size distribution of CQDs, and the particle sizes of CQDs are all less than 4 nm, mostly in the range of 3.6–3.7 nm. The HRTEM image (Fig. 3d) shows that the lattice stripe of CQDs powder is 0.23 nm, which is the same as that reported in other literatures [34].

The cross sections of the (GO/CQDs)n films are observed by SEM (Fig. 4a -e). It is known that GO and CQDs layers are well adhered to Si substrate with high density and uniform thickness. The thickness of the films increases gradually with the increasing number of the assembled layers, and (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer possesses a maximum thickness of 99 nm. Single-component GO film (designated as (GO)5) with the similar thickness of 96 nm to (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer is also prepared for comparation (Fig. 4f).

The surface morphology and roughness of the films are analyzed by AFM (Fig. 5 ). The results show that some wrinkles are discontinuously distributed on the surface of the single-component (GO)5 film, and the root-mean-square (RMS) roughness is 4.67 nm over a scanning range of 20 μm × 20 μm. The surfaces of (GO/CQDs)n composite multilayers are scattered with microprotrusion. The amount of the microprotrusion increases with the increased layers. The RMS roughness is 2.29, 3.89, 4.36, 9.20, and 10.64 nm, respectively, for (GO/CQDs)n (n = 1–5) composite multilayers. The RMS roughness of (GO)5 film is similar to that of (GO/CQDs)3 film and much smaller than that of (GO/CQDs)4 and (GO/CQDs)5 films.

XPS was used to analyze the surface composition and the chemical states of (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films. As shown in Fig. 6a , the peaks of O 1 s, N 1 s, C 1 s, Si 2 s and Si 2p were found in the full spectra of (GO/CQDs)n films. The N 1 s was from the adhesive layer APTES SAM containing -NH2 groups. And the peaks of Si 2 s and Si 2p were from the silicon substrate. The Si signals weakened with the increase number of the assembled layers, which confirmed the successful assembly of each layer of the films. The fine spectra of C 1 s region of (GO/CQDs)1, (GO/CQDs)3 and (GO/CQDs)5 films (Fig. 6b-6d) could be deconvoluted into C=C/C–C, C–O, C=O, and O=C–OH bands at 284.8, 286.3, 287.1, and 288.9 eV, respectively [35,36]. The relative content of C–O from the C 1 s fitting results was 16.95 %, 20.47 % and 37.97 % for (GO/CQDs)1, (GO/CQDs)3 and (GO/CQDs)5 films. The content of C–O increased with the increasing number of (GO/CQDs)n films, which indicated that GO and CQDs could form chemical grafting in the films by the esterification reaction.

Fig. 7 shows the Raman spectra of (GO/CQDs)n films. The D band at 1346 cm−1 and the G band at 1600 cm−1 were observed. With the increasing number of the films, the intensity of the D and G bands increased, and the peaks of the D and G bands became sharper. It indicates that GO and CQDs were successfully assembled to form (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films, and the well-stacked GO-GO layered structures were still maintained in (GO/CQDs)n multilayer films [14]. The layered structure may be conducive to reducing the friction and wear of the multilayer films. The intensity ratio of D to G peaks (ID/IG) of (GO/CQDs)1–5 films was 1.02, 1.19, 1.26, 1.33, and 1.41, respectively (Fig. 7b-7f). As the number of (GO/CQDs)n layers increased, the ID/IG of the thin films slightly increased, which indicates an increase in the degree of GO functionalization and disorder [37,38]. It may be due to the introduction of CQDs between the neighboring GO layers, leading to the functionalization of GO and increased disorder.

The friction tests were carried out under the conditions of 4 N and 1 Hz in a ball-on-plate reciprocating mode, and the friction coefficient curves of (GO/CQDs)n films were shown in Fig. 8 . The (GO/CQDs)1 film exhibited limited wear resistance. It was worn out after about 100 s sliding, witnessed by the sudden increase of the friction coefficient. With the increasing number of (GO/CQDs)n multilayers, the anti-wear life of the films were gradually improved. Significantly, the (GO/CQDs)5 film possessed excellent tribological properties under the high load. The service life is more than 9500 s and the friction coefficient is 0.17 for (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer. (GO)5 film was prepared and tested under the same conditions to evaluate the effect of CQDs in the multilayer films. The friction coefficient of (GO)5 film increased rapidly near 1400s and the film failed. The service life of (GO/CQDs)5 film is more than six times than that of (GO)5 film. The small size and the graphic fragment structure characteristic of CQDs play important role for the improvement of the lubrication performance [25].

The worn surfaces of (GO/CQDs)n films were examined with SEM, as shown in Fig. 9 . It can be clearly seen that the furrows in the center of the wear area are clearly reduced and become smoother with the increasing number of assembly layers. Furthermore, the wear rates of (GO/CQDs)n films significantly decreased with the growth layers, especially using (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer film as solid lubricant. The wear rates of the films with 1–5 layers are 2.16×10−5, 3.15×10−6, 2.80×10−6, 1.49×10−6, and 3.76×10−7 mm3/N·m, respectively. The wear rate of (GO)5 film is 2.61×10−6 mm3/N m. It was obvious that the wear rate of (GO/CQDs)5 film is about 1/50 of that of (GO/CQDs)1 film. Compared with (GO)5 film, the wear rate of (GO/CQDs)5 film is reduced by 85 %. The excellent anti-wear ability of (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer under the high load could be ascribed to the unique structure of the film, which presents a layered three-dimensional structure composed of GO nanosheets alternate with CQDs. The rigid GO nanosheets is conducive to improving the load-carrying capacity and anti-wear properties. Meanwhile, CQDs existing the neighboring layers exhibit excellent deformation resistance and crack resistance, which is helpful to form tough friction films [39].

To further study the anti-friction and anti-wear mechanism of (GO/CQDs)5 films, ex-situ Raman mapping and Raman spectra of the contact regions at different friction periods were analyzed, as show in Fig. 10 . Before rubbing, the D and G bands on the entire surface of (GO/CQDs)5 film showed relatively high strength, and the predominant color of the contact regions was blue. The strength of the D and G bands in the contact regions slightly decreased with the friction process. After sliding for 600 s, some contact regions became blue together with light blue. After sliding for 3600 s, the color of the most region gradually changed to green. When the friction time further increased to 7200 s, some contact regions became yellow together with light pink. After sliding for 9500 s, most of the contact regions became red. Obviously, the strength of the D and G bands gradually decreases after experiencing a long-time friction, and the thin film loses efficacy gradually. It has a good agreement with the friction curve. Meanwhile, the value of ID/IG is first decreases and then increases with the friction process (Fig. 10c). It indicates the reduced disorder in the initial stage and then increased disorder in the later stage. That is, the GO layer in the thin film undergoes in situ structural transformation in the initial stage to yield graphene structure under friction force [40]. Such a structural transformation is benefit for achieving stable and low friction. The yield graphene structure gradually turned into amorphous carbon in later stage, resulting in a decrease in lubrication effect and tribological performance [41].

Based on the above experimental results, the friction mechanism of (GO/CQDs)5 thin film is shown in Fig. 11 . The wear process of (GO/CQDs)5 film mainly goes through three stages. The structure of the film is compact and uniform, and CQDs exists between adjacent GO layers. During friction, CQDs existing the neighboring GO layers exhibit excellent deformation resistance and crack resistance, which is helpful to form tough friction films. Meanwhile, the small size and the graphic fragment structure characteristic of CQDs play important role for the improvement of the lubrication performance. In addition, friction-induced structural transformation makes some GO convert into graphene on sliding interface, which is benefit for reducing friction. After experiencing a long-time friction, the CQDs and GO in the friction area are consumed in large quantities, and the remained CQDs and GO are not enough to bear the severe friction under the high load of 4 N, resulting in the film failure. Therefore, (GO/CQDs)5 composite multilayer composed of GO nanosheets alternate with CQDs possesses excellent wear resistance with long wear life under the high load of 4 N.

(GO/CQDs)n multilayer films (n = 1–5) were constructed on APTES-SAM modified silicon wafers by spin-coating method. The macroscopic tribological behaviors of (GO/CQDs)n films were studied under the load of 4 N and frequency of 1 Hz. The results show that (GO/CQDs)5 multilayer film exhibits excellent anti-wear properties with the wear life of 9400 s, which is more than six times than that of (GO)5 film with the same thickness. The outstanding friction performance could be ascribed to the distinctive structure composed of GO nanosheets alternate with CQDs. Wherein, GO nanosheets effectively enhance the load-carrying capacity and anti-wear properties. Meanwhile, CQDs could be helpful to form tough friction films and improve the lubrication performance. In addition, friction-induced in situ structural transformation makes some GO convert into graphene on sliding interface, which is beneficial to achieving stable and low friction. Therefore, (GO/CQDs)5 film is expected to be good lubricating films for MEMS and other microdevices.

Li Chen: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition, Data curation. Hang Zhu: Investigation, Formal analysis, Visualization. Jingyuan Yang: Investigation. Jianxiang Lv: Validation, Investigation. Xingkai Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Changning Bai: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 51765036) and the Foundation of Liaoning Key Laboratory of Aero-engine Material Tribology (Grant No. LKLAMTF202303) for financial support.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.surfin.2024.104071.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Huang, A. Sai Sarathi Vasan, R. Doraiswami, M. Osterman, M. Pecht, MEMS reliability review, IEEE Trans. Device Mater. Reliab. 2012; 12(2): 482.
S.H. Kim, D.B. Asay, M.T. Dugger, Nanotribology and MEMS, Nano Today 2007; 2(5): 22.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tribology on the small scale: a bottom up approach to friction, lubrication, and wear

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hainsworth, Tribology on the small scale: a bottom up approach to friction, lubrication, and wear, J. Phys. A Math. Theor. 2008; 41(47): 479002.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Atomic-layer deposition of wear-resistant coatings for microelectromechanical devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T.M. Mayer, J.W. Elam, S.M. George, P.G. Kotula, R.S. Goeke, Atomic-layer deposition of wear-resistant coatings for microelectromechanical devices, Appl. Phys. Lett. 2003; 82(17): 2883.
R. Maboudian, C. Carraro, Surface chemistry and tribology of MEMS, Annu. Rev. Phys. Chem. 2004; 55(1): 35.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface topography evolution and fatigue fracture in polysilicon MEMS structures

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S.M. Allameh, P. Shrotriya, A. Butterwick, S.B. Brown, W.O. Soboyejo, Surface topography evolution and fatigue fracture in polysilicon MEMS structures, J. Microelectromech. Syst. 2003; 12(3): 313.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Self-assembly of octadecyltrichlorosilane on graphene oxide and the tribological performances of the resultant film

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.F. Ou, Y. Wang, J.Q. Wang, S. Liu, Z.P. Li, S.R. Yang, Self-assembly of octadecyltrichlorosilane on graphene oxide and the tribological performances of the resultant film, J. Phys. Chem. C 2011; 115(20): 10080.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Study of mixed Langmuir–Blodgett films of hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon amphiphilic compounds by scanning surface potential microscopy and friction force microscopy

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: K. Yagi, M. Fujihira, Study of mixed Langmuir–Blodgett films of hydrocarbon and fluorocarbon amphiphilic compounds by scanning surface potential microscopy and friction force microscopy, Appl. Surf. Sci. 2000; 157(4): 405.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tribological behavior of grafted polymer gel nanocoatings

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: D. Julthongpiput, H.S. Ahn, D.I. Kim, V.V. Tsukruk, Tribological behavior of grafted polymer gel nanocoatings, Tribol. Lett. 2002; 13(1): 35.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Chemical vapor deposition-grown graphene: the thinnest solid lubricant

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tribological properties of low-temperature graphite fluorides. Influence of the structure on the lubricating performances
